coredata.dc:title: Luminescence property of phosphoramidic acid oligomer nanodots in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lu
Liu, Zheng-Fei
Jun Jin, Wei
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent polymer dots have showed great potential applications in chemical sensing and bioimaging. Herein, phosphoramidic acid oligomers in aqueous solution can form nanodots (ONDs) with mean diameter of 50 ~ 60 nm. The ONDs display blue fluorescence of excitation-dependence and the fluorescence quantum yields can reach 4.16% to 9.71% under the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths. The steady and dynamic fluorescence quenching experiments by iodide ion show that the luminescence of ONDs originates to charge transfer (CT). The calculation on distribution of HOMO and LUMO of ONDs in monomer and dimer states supports further the CT mechanism, and the separated and localized distribution of HOMO and LUMO provides possible explanation on the excitation wavelength dependent luminescence based on internal and external CT. It is expected that the luminescent ONDS are useful in chemical or biological sensing fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphoramidic acid oligomers
Oligomer nanodots
Fluorescence
Quenching
Iodine ion
Charge transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphologies of three oligomers in aqueous solution
Absorption and fluorescent spectroscopy of three oligomers in aqueous solution
The luminescence quenching and decay in the presence of iodide ion

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of P-XY (a), P-ND (b) and P-BAC (c).
FTIR spectra of P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC in solids. The symbol “L” means the samples from aqueous solution by dry procedure.

Fluorescence photos under UV-365 nm and fluorescence emission spectra of P-XY (a), P-ND (b) and P-BAC (c) corresponding various concentrations in aqueous solution (insets refer to plots of fluorescence intensity ratio v.s. the concentrations, where I 0 refers to the intensity at 0.4 mg/mL). The concentration serials from left to right for P-XY (λ ex 339 nm) 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.1, 6.3, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300 mg/mL; for P-ND (λ ex 345 nm) 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.1, 6.3, 12.5, 25, 50, 75, 100, 200, 400 mg/mL; for P-BAC (λ ex 325 nm) 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.1, 6.3, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 500 mg/mL.

(a) Typical quenching spectra of 160 mg/mL P-ND solution by iodide ion. (b) Typical quenching curves (obeying I 0/I = 1 + K SV[Q], where I 0 refers to the intensity in the absence of iodide) of P-ND, P-BAC and P-XY solutions by iodide ion.

Typical decay profiles of P-XY(a), P-ND(b) and P-BAC(c) solutions in the absence and presence of iodide ion. (d) Stern-Volmer plots (obeying τ 0/τ = 1 + τ 0 k q[Q] , where τ 0 and τ refer to the average lifetime in the absence and presence of iodide respectively) for P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC.

The orbital distributions of upper-HOMO and lower-LUMO of the optimized structural units in dimer for P-ND (di-phosphordiamidic unit, 2 phosphoryls with 4-amide bonds) (a-b) and (di-phosphortriamidic unit, 2 phosphoryls with 6-amide bonds) (c-d).
Proposed structural units of oligomer P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC.
The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths, quantum yields and Stern-Volmer quenching parameters by iodide ion in aqueous solution (aq).
The energy gaps ΔE between HOMO and LUMO and possible noncovalent interaction.

row.entry: Hydrogen bond and vdW contact parameters

head.ce:title: Luminescence property of phosphoramidic acid oligomer nanodots in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong Shanxi, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphordi- and phosphortriamidic acid oligomers aggregate to nanodots with fluorescence of excitation-wavelength-dependence in aqueous solution.

Luminescent polymer dots have showed great potential applications in chemical sensing and bioimaging. Herein, phosphoramidic acid oligomers in aqueous solution can form nanodots (ONDs) with mean diameter of 50 ~ 60 nm. The ONDs display blue fluorescence of excitation-dependence and the fluorescence quantum yields can reach 4.16% to 9.71% under the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths. The steady and dynamic fluorescence quenching experiments by iodide ion show that the luminescence of ONDs originates to charge transfer (CT). The calculation on distribution of HOMO and LUMO of ONDs in monomer and dimer states supports further the CT mechanism, and the separated and localized distribution of HOMO and LUMO provides possible explanation on the excitation wavelength dependent luminescence based on internal and external CT. It is expected that the luminescent ONDS are useful in chemical or biological sensing fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductive quantum dots (QDs) [1–3] and carbon dots have special fluorescence properties, and show great potential or practical applications in various fields, especially in bioimaging [4–9]. However, recently, a kind of luminescent material named polymer dots (P-dots) or polymer carbon dots has aroused great attentions [10,11]. Among them, non-conjugated P-dots are of particular interest due to their luminescence originates from non-classical luminophors. In general, the non-conjugated polymers have no or very weak fluorescence due to their non-conjugated structures. However, some non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) showed very strong emission, even that could be observed by naked eye. The luminescence phenomenon of non-conjugated polymer dots is attributed to the crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect by some researchers [12,13]. That is, the luminescence of non-conjugated fluorophores can be enhanced by chemical crosslinking or physical immobilization of polymer chains.

However, unfortunately, above explanation on mechanism does not involve in the electronic origin of luminescence. So, Sun et al [14] proposed that the photoluminescence of tertiary aliphatic amines as a fluorescent probe to cell imaging may originate from the oxygenamine “contact” donor − acceptor sp3N → O complex formed in the oxidation of aliphatic tertiary amine based on electron donor − acceptor interaction. Also, Jeong et al [15] reported that the tunable emission from red to blue by adjusting both the excitation wavelengths of P-dots and their concentration in an aqueous solution may be relative to energy transfer or charge transfer in poly(propylene glycol)-rich core. Vallan et al [16] made important progress in the exploration of luminescence mechanism. The experimental and calculation show that the photoinduced charge transfer between amide moiety (−CONH−, HOMO) and carboxylic groups (−COOH, LUMU) in P-dots consisting of polyamide chains of about 10 monomer units thus constitutes the origin of the strong blue fluorescence emission. Moreover, hydrogen-bond-mediated supramolecular interactions between the polyamide chains enabling a rigid network structure further contribute to the enhancement of the radiative process [16].

The application of non-conjugated polymer materials also is of interest in chemical or biological aspects. For example, Tong et al [17] used the crosslink enhanced emission of non-conjugated polymer dots by polyurethane polymerization of triazine derivatives to the selective recognition of Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching. The quenching reason may be due to the paramagnetic property of Fe3+ ion and its complex with polymer that can change the electron distribution of triazine CEE unit. Yu et al [18] reported that chitosan derivatives can be charred by hydrothermal method to carbon dots and the materials was used to sensing Pd2+ by the static fluorescence quenching with a detection limit of 63.3 nmol/L. Yang et al [19] used non-conjugated polymer carbon dots to determine the drug metronidazole by fluorescence quenching with a detection limit of 20 ng/mL and the method was applied to milk samples with good recovery.

Sun et al [20] prepared non-conjugated polyethyleneimine (PEI) PDs modified by targeting molecules (folic acid) for a new generation of bio-fluorophores. In the system, the free folic acid can quench the luminescence of PDs by energy transfer, while the conjugated folic acid could maintain the luminescence to a certain degree which implies the potential applications in targeted bioimaging. Zhang et al [21] reported a semiconducting conjugated polymer dots P-dots and they are compartmentalized in liposomes as photocatalyst for in situ hydrogen therapy. This is an important application in biological medicine.

It can be seen from the above discussion that non-conjugated luminescent polymers do show their usefulness, and the luminescence mechanism still needs to be further explored. Previously, the phosphoramidic acid oligomers, P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC, are designed and prepared to develop phosphorescent materials with ultralong phosphorescence lifetime [22]. Herein, a further investigation notices that the oligomers in aqueous solution can form nanodots, named oligomer nanodots (abbreviated as ONDs), and the ONDs are fluorescent in aqueous solution under UV radiation. As non-conjugation luminescence polymer systems, the most of reports consists of carbonyl groups in the references. The novelty of the oligomers is herein that, compared with carbonyl, phosphoryl not only is a group to participate in luminescence, but also has one more covalent bond than carbonyl which can be used to tune luminescence systems or change intermolecular interaction between phosphoryls, or phosphoryl and other components. Moreover, the hydrophobic group xylyl, bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane and cyclohexyl is conducive to aggregation to form luminescent ONDs. The paper is try study the luminescence property and mechanism of ONDs in aqueous solution by spectroscopy methods combining with scanning and transmission electron microscopy (SEM/TEM) image, and theoretical calculation. It is expected that the results are helpful to support the further application of the luminescence ONDs in chemical or biological sensing fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphologies of three oligomers in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para:
The oligomers are actually the mixture of phosphordiamidic and phosphortriamidic acid units and their possible structures are shown in Scheme 1
[22].

Due to the polar groups such as P = O, –OH and –NH2, P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC all have good hydrophilicity, the solubility in aqueous solution can reach 300, 400 and 500 mg/mL, respectively, and it is equivalent to phosphoramidic acid unit of about 10-2 mol/L.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are performed to further characterize the morphological property of the oligomer nanodots. As shown in Fig. 1 , SEM images show that three ONDs appears in irregular spherical particles with mean diameter of 50 ~ 60 nm. TEM images of three ONDs seem to be statistically consistent with SEM, as shown in Figure S1 .

Fig. 2 represents the FTIR spectra of P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC samples in solid. The frequencies at about 3420 cm−1 and 3270 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H, respectively. The absorption bands of O-H and N-H become particularly broad profile without very obvious peaks due to the presence of hydrogen bonds. Meanwhile, the N-H bending vibration appears at 1624 ~ 1640 cm−1. The positions of ν (C–N) and ν(P–N) are located at 1124 ~ 1130 cm−1, and 1070 ~ 1040 cm−1, 930 ~ 950 cm−1, respectively [23,24]. The N–O–H⋅⋅⋅O=P hydrogen bonding should lower obviously the frequency of ν (P=O). The 870, 805 and 705 cm−1 absorption peaks of P-XY are assigned to the ν(C-H) of benzene, which proves the meta-substitution of benzene ring.

As shown in Figure S2 , due to the interference of strong fluorescence background, the information provided by Raman spectra is very limited. Some obvious peaks are assigned tentatively as follows. The O-H stretching vibration is very weak in Raman spectrum, which appears about 3660–3640 cm−1. However, C-H stretching is more prominent between 2960 and 2850 cm−1. The 1606 ~ 1619 cm−1 may be due to the bending mode of N-H. The P-N bond vibration should be at about 1430 cm−1. The origination of ∼1845 cm−1 is not clear, probably from C-C, which does not appear in FTIR spectra (Fig. 2).

As shown in Figure S3 , obviously, the band of O1s is obviously asymmetry with half full width about 3 eV. The band can be divided into two peaks in Gaussian distribution, localized at 531.5 ~ 531.9 eV and 529.7 ~ 529.9 eV, respectively. They may come from P = O and P-O-H or their hydrogen bonding species. The peak of N1s is symmetry localized at 401.9 ~ 401.27 eV with half full width about 1.66 eV. The peak of C1s is asymmetry. The peak of P2p is asymmetry with half full width about 1.8 eV. The peak can be divided into two in Gaussian distribution. One at higher bonding energy 133 eV may be relative to P = O group, and other at lower bonding energy 132 eV may be relative to P = O⋅⋅⋅H or P-N/O⋅⋅⋅H hydrogen bonding [25].

The above results indicate that oligomeric chains of P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC composed of phosphoramidic acid units and different spacer groups can form micro-orderly aggregates in aqueous solution due to the hydrogen bonds to microcross-linking and possible hydrophobic interaction between spacers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and fluorescent spectroscopy of three oligomers in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figure S4 , all three oligomers display strong absorption in solution. For P-XY, the absorption at 260 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition of P = O group, the absorption at 192 and 207 nm should originate from the π-π* transition of benzene ring and P = O group. The n-π* transition is localized to P = O group in dilution solution. The absorption of P-BAC and P-ND at 190 nm should originates from π-π* transition of P = O, the trailing absorption after 200 nm, or weak absorption at about 250 nm is from n-π* transition of P = O. Comparatively, the absorption of P-BAC and P-ND at 190 nm does not display a peak like P-XY., which should be due to that P-BAC and P-ND contain solely P = O but P-XY both phenyl and P = O group. In addition, charge transfer transition occurs probably between separated HOMO and LUMO, cf. the calculation section later.

The concentration effect of three oligomers aqueous solutions on fluorescence is obvious. The dilution solution of three oligomers looks transparent and luminescence is very weak under UV-365 nm. But the luminescence enhances gradually as the concentration increases progressively although the solutions are still transparent and viscous. As shown in Fig. 3 , represented by P-ND, the luminescence intensity increases remarkably with the increase of concentration from 0.4 mg/mL at a fixed excitation wavelength. When the P-ND concentration is increased to 400 mg/mL the luminescence intensity of the P-ND aqueous solution reaches the maximum, and the luminescence quantum yield is 7.24%, as listed in Table 1 . The plots of fluorescence intensity ratio v.s. the concentrations of three oligomeric aqueous solutions first increase rapidly and then reach or approach a platform at 100 mg/mL for P-XY, 200 mg/mL for P-ND, 300 mg/mL for P-BAC, respectively. It means that the solubility of P-XY is the lowest and P-BAC is the highest due to difference of space groups, phenyl, bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane and cyclohexyl.

In addition, as shown in Figure S5 , the luminescence of three oligomers in aggregation states in solution has the property of excitation-wavelength-dependence, which are derived from different emission components. That is, actually, anyone of the three oligomers is a mixture of phosphordiamidic and phosphortriamidic acid units, and also size of ONDs by hydrogen bonds of different number is somewhat different [22]. For luminescence carbon dots or polymer dots this seems a common phenomenon [4–9].

On luminescence mechanism, the photo-induced charge transfer between groups is much believable [16]. While the nature of the clustering-triggered emission should be understood as clustering-enhanced charge transfer [26,27]. Here, n-π* transition transfer occurs within ONDs in one phosphoramidic unit or between phosphoramidic acid groups. In dilute solution, the luminescence is weak due to large separation of phosphoramidic acids themselves in space as well as collision to water, vibration or rotation of groups. When the concentration increases, aggregates of phosphoramidic acid units form by intramolecular interaction, and emission is enhanced due to the increased degree of orbital overlap between n- and π*-orbitals of O-P, P = O, N-P groups, as well as efficient charge transfer and isolated contact with the environment. The proposed luminescence mechanism will be discussed further in the following calculation section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The luminescence quenching and decay in the presence of iodide ion
ce:section.ce:para:
The I- quenching experiments are often used to explore intermolecular interactions [28–31]. Fig. 4
(a) shows typically that the fluorescence from P-ND ONDs can be quenched by iodine ions, also as shown in Figure S6 for P-XY and P-BAC. It can be seen that P-ND fluorescence is gradually reduced with the increase of I- concentration. Fig. 4 (b) shows the Stern-Volmer quenching curves of three substances, and the corresponding bimolecular quenching constants (K SV) are 21.4 (P-ND), 25.7 (P-BAC) and 7.57 (P-XY) M−1. It shows that fluorescence quenching of P-ND ONDs is the most significant. It is assumed that I- ions are more likely to diffuse close to or enter the P-ND ONDs. On the one hand, the hydrogen bond between phosphoramide units is destroyed by I-, which makes the ONDs disintegrated partially and the fluorescence reduced. The iodide I- is an important hole quenching reagent [32] and the charge-transfer from I- to N-P = O group [16], on the other hand, deactivates the excited state, the following fluorescent decay measurements with the increase of I- concentration support the charge transfer mechanism. (cf the k q later).

In order to further explain the presumption that iodine ion destroys the aggregates and intensifies the quenching effect, following experiments are further completed. The double dilution of solution in which the concentration of ONDs decreases and the quenching should be intensified ( Figure S7
a and b). Indeed, the quenching constant is 33.3 (P-ND) M−1, which is 1.6 times higher of 21.4 (P-ND) M−1 as mentioned above.

The decay kinetics of fluorescent quenching also is investigated. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescent decay profiles in the absence and presence of iodide ion and Stern-Volmer plots for P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC. Table 1 gives the quenching rate constants. By comparing K SV as described above and τ 0 k q values in Fig. 5, it can be concluded that dynamic quenching is predominant absolutely in quenching process. This demonstrates further validity of the charge transfer mechanism. It can be speculated that the iodide ions are more favorable for charge transfer than simply destroying the ONDs by breaking hydrogen bonding among phosphordiamidic or phosphortriamidic units of oligomers.
In short, the aggregation behavior of three oligomers in solution and the origin of their luminescence are further proved by I- ion quenching experiments.

In order to understand the luminescent mechanism of three ONDs, the calculation is completed using P-ND as a typical example. Considering the speed of calculation, the monomers of phosphordiamide and -triamide, and their dimer are set as calculation units,respectively, as shown in Scheme S1. It is expected that these calculation units can represent the real experimental systems.

For the monomers, as shown in Figure S8
(a-b), HOMO and LUMO is not separated, localizing on phosphoryl groups (N-P = O or -P = O). However, as shown in Figure S8 (c-d), the HOMO and LUMO orbitals in the phosphortriamide unit are separated in space in different degrees. The HOMO orbital is mainly located on the amino group, and the LUMO orbital is mainly located on the phosphoryl group. For the dimers, as shown in Figure S9 and Fig. 6 , the HOMO and LUMO orbitals in both phosphordiamide and phosphortriamide units are separated in space in different degrees. The energy gaps between HOMO and LUMO are listed in Table 2 . The internal charge transfers from HOMO to LUMO in N-P = O unit (named internal CT) or external one from HOMO on –NH2 to LUMO on N-P = O or P = O unit ((named external CT) should occur. That is, this kind of charge transfer mechanism of OND luminescence is consistent with reference [16]. Moreover, this charge transfer mechanism based on orbital separation and localization, as well as diversity of separation energy ΔE are also one of the important reasons for excitation wavelength dependent luminescence.

In addition, as shown in Figure S8, S9 and Fig. 6, except in phosphordiamidic unit with four phosphoryls where no hydrogen bonding is displayed in the chain which stretches linearly due to the small steric hindrance, some hydrogen bond interactions exist in diamide or triamide units, for example, O–H···O = P hydrogen bond in dimer of mono-phosphortriamide with H···O distance of 1.50596 Å and bonding angle of 177.724°, which is -44.63% shorter than the sum of van deer Waals radius of interacting atoms. Other HB or near van deer Waals contacts between H and O/N atoms are listed inTable 2.

The phosphoramidic acid oligomers, P-XY, P-ND and P-BAC, can aggregate as ONDs by hydrophobicity of space groups and hydrogen bonding between phosphoramidic acid units in aqueous solution. They produce the fluorescence of excitation-wavelength-dependence with fluorescence decay of ns scale as well as absolute quantum yield of close to 5–10%. The origination of fluorescence of the oligomer ONDs in solution is relative to the charge transfer within P = O or N-P = O groups, as well as between -P = O and –N– and affected by hydrogen bonding [16,22], resembling to the cluster-trigged emission of non-conjugated systems [23,24]. The luminescence nature of the ONDs are further demonstrated by iodide quenching experiments and calculation chemistry. The paper implies the potential application of this kind of polymer nanodots in chemosensor or biological tracer in future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974011) and Natural Science Foundation of ShanXi (201801D121035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119261.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
